data.content:
### REST API endpoints for collaborators

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/rest/projects-classic/collaborators.md

Endpoints for managing collaborators on a Project, including adding, removing, and listing collaborators.

```APIDOC
## GET /projects/{project_id}/collaborators

### Description
Lists the collaborators for a project.

### Method
GET

### Endpoint
/projects/{project_id}/collaborators

### Parameters
#### Path Parameters
- **project_id** (integer) - Required - The unique identifier of the project.

### Request Example
(No request body for GET requests)

### Response
#### Success Response (200)
- **collaborators** (array) - A list of collaborator objects.
  - **login** (string) - The username of the collaborator.
  - **id** (integer) - The user ID of the collaborator.
  - **node_id** (string) - The GraphQL node ID of the collaborator.
  - **url** (string) - The URL to the collaborator's profile.
  - **html_url** (string) - The URL to the collaborator's HTML profile.
  - **type** (string) - The type of collaborator (e.g., 'user').
  - **permissions** (object) - The permissions the collaborator has on the project.
    - **read** (boolean) - Whether the collaborator can read the project.
    - **write** (boolean) - Whether the collaborator can write to the project.
    - **admin** (boolean) - Whether the collaborator has admin permissions on the project.

#### Response Example
{
  "collaborators": [
    {
      "login": "octocat",
      "id": 1,
      "node_id": "MDQ6VXNlcjE=",
      "url": "https://api.github.com/users/octocat",
      "html_url": "https://github.com/octocat",
      "type": "user",
      "permissions": {
        "read": true,
        "write": true,
        "admin": true
      }
    }
  ]
}

## PUT /projects/{project_id}/collaborators/{username}

### Description
Adds a collaborator to a project.

### Method
PUT

### Endpoint
/projects/{project_id}/collaborators/{username}

### Parameters
#### Path Parameters
- **project_id** (integer) - Required - The unique identifier of the project.
- **username** (string) - Required - The username of the collaborator to add.

#### Request Body
- **permission** (string) - Optional - The permission level to grant to the collaborator. Can be one of 'read', 'write', or 'admin'. Defaults to 'write'.

### Request Example
```json
{
  "permission": "admin"
}
```

### Response
#### Success Response (204)
No content.

#### Response Example
(No response body for 204 No Content)

## DELETE /projects/{project_id}/collaborators/{username}

### Description
Removes a collaborator from a project.

### Method
DELETE

### Endpoint
/projects/{project_id}/collaborators/{username}

### Parameters
#### Path Parameters
- **project_id** (integer) - Required - The unique identifier of the project.
- **username** (string) - Required - The username of the collaborator to remove.

### Request Example
(No request body for DELETE requests)

### Response
#### Success Response (204)
No content.

#### Response Example
(No response body for 204 No Content)
```

--------------------------------

### About repository invitations

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/rest/collaborators/invitations.md

This section provides an overview of the repository invitations API. It explains how to view and manage invitations to collaborate on a repository. Invited users can accept or decline these invitations. It also clarifies the difference between adding a collaborator and inviting one, and details the required OAuth scopes.

```APIDOC
## About repository invitations

You can view and manage invitations to collaborate on a repository. The invited users (or external services on behalf of invited users) can choose to accept or decline the invitations.

To add a user as a collaborator, use the Collaborators endpoints instead. For more information, see [AUTOTITLE](/rest/collaborators/collaborators#add-a-repository-collaborator).

Note that the `repo:invite` [OAuth scope](/apps/oauth-apps/building-oauth-apps/scopes-for-oauth-apps) grants targeted access to invitations **without** also granting access to repository code, while the `repo` scope grants permission to code as well as invitations.
```

--------------------------------

### Add Guest Collaborator Role to Azure AD App Manifest

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/admin/managing-accounts-and-repositories/managing-users-in-your-enterprise/enabling-guest-collaborators.md

This JSON snippet shows how to add the 'Guest Collaborator' role to the appRoles section of your Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) application manifest. This enables the role to be assigned to users for guest collaboration in your enterprise. Ensure the 'id' matches the one provided in the documentation to avoid errors.

```json
{
  "allowedMemberTypes": [
    "User"
  ],
  "description": "Guest Collaborator",
  "displayName": "Guest Collaborator",
  "id": "1ebc4a02-e56c-43a6-92a5-02ee09b90824",
  "isEnabled": true,
  "lang": null,
  "origin": "Application",
  "value": null
}
```

--------------------------------

### User Interactions API

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/rest/interactions/user.md

Manage interaction limits for users on GitHub. This API allows you to control the behavior of different user types (existing, contributor, collaborator) in your public repositories.

```APIDOC
## About User Interactions

You can use the REST API to temporarily restrict which type of user can comment, open issues, or create pull requests on your public repositories. This documentation outlines the general capabilities for managing user interaction limits.

### Purpose
To control the interaction levels of different user types (existing, contributor, collaborator) on public repositories.

### Scope
This API manages user-level interaction limits, which override repository-level limits for repositories owned by the user.

### Related Endpoints
For repository-specific interaction limits, refer to the [Repository Interactions API](/rest/interactions/repos).

### User Types
*   **Existing User:** Users who have already committed to your repository.
*   **Contributor User:** Users who have already contributed to your repository.
*   **Collaborator User:** Users who have already collaborated on your repository.
```

--------------------------------

### Add Copilot as an assignee using `addAssigneesToAssignable` mutation (shell)

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/copilot/how-tos/use-copilot-agents/coding-agent/create-a-pr.md

This shell command demonstrates how to use the `addAssigneesToAssignable` mutation to add Copilot to an issue without removing existing assignees. It requires the issue ID and bot ID, and supports the `agentAssignment` object for task customization. This is useful for collaborative assignment scenarios.

```shell
gh api graphql -f query='mutation {
  addAssigneesToAssignable(input: {
    assignableId: "ISSUE_ID",
    assigneeIds: ["BOT_ID"],
    agentAssignment: {
      targetRepositoryId: "REPOSITORY_ID",
      baseRef: "main",
      customInstructions: "Collaborate on this task",
      customAgent: "",
      model: ""
    }
  }) {
    assignable {
      ... on Issue {
        id
        title
        assignees(first: 10) {
          nodes {
            login
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}' -H 'GraphQL-Features: issues_copilot_assignment_api_support,coding_agent_model_selection'
```

--------------------------------

### Add Assignees using addAssigneesToAssignable

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/copilot/how-tos/use-copilot-agents/coding-agent/create-a-pr.md

Adds {% data variables.product.prodname_copilot_short %} to an existing issue as an assignee, preserving other assignees, using the `addAssigneesToAssignable` mutation.

```APIDOC
## POST /graphql/mutation/addAssigneesToAssignable

### Description
Adds {% data variables.product.prodname_copilot_short %} as an assignee to an issue without removing existing assignees.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
/graphql

### Parameters
#### Request Body
- **query** (string) - Required - The GraphQL mutation string.
- **variables** (object) - Optional - Variables for the mutation.
  - **input** (object) - Required
    - **assignableId** (string) - Required - The GraphQL ID of the issue.
    - **assigneeIds** (array of strings) - Required - An array containing the Bot ID of {% data variables.product.prodname_copilot_short %}.
    - **agentAssignment** (object) - Optional - Configuration for the Copilot agent.
      - **targetRepositoryId** (string) - Required - The GraphQL ID of the repository.
      - **baseRef** (string) - Required - The base branch name.
      - **customInstructions** (string) - Optional - Custom instructions for the agent.
      - **customAgent** (string) - Optional - Specify a custom agent.
      - **model** (string) - Optional - Specify the model to use.

### Request Example
```shell
gh api graphql -f query='mutation { addAssigneesToAssignable(input: { assignableId: "ISSUE_ID", assigneeIds: ["BOT_ID"], agentAssignment: { targetRepositoryId: "REPOSITORY_ID", baseRef: "main", customInstructions: "Collaborate on this task" } }) { assignable { ... on Issue { id title assignees(first: 10) { nodes { login } } } } } }' -H 'GraphQL-Features: issues_copilot_assignment_api_support,coding_agent_model_selection'
```

### Response
#### Success Response (200)
- **data.assignable.id** (string) - The GraphQL ID of the issue.
- **data.assignable.title** (string) - The title of the issue.
- **data.assignable.assignees.nodes** (array) - A list of assignees for the issue.

#### Response Example
```json
{
  "data": {
    "assignable": {
      "id": "ISSUE_ID",
      "title": "Example Issue Title",
      "assignees": {
        "nodes": [
          {
            "login": "existing-assignee"
          },
          {
            "login": "copilot-swe-agent"
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```
```

--------------------------------

### Adding assignees to an existing issue

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/copilot/how-tos/use-copilot-agents/coding-agent/create-a-pr.md

This endpoint allows you to add assignees to an existing issue in a repository.

```APIDOC
## POST /repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/ISSUE_NUMBER/assignees

### Description
Adds one or more assignees to an existing issue.

### Method
POST

### Endpoint
`/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues/{issue_number}/assignees`

### Parameters
#### Path Parameters
- **owner** (string) - Required - The owner of the repository.
- **repo** (string) - Required - The name of the repository.
- **issue_number** (integer) - Required - The number of the issue.

#### Query Parameters
None

#### Request Body
- **assignees** (array of strings) - Required - A list of GitHub usernames to assign to the issue.
- **agent_assignment** (object) - Optional - Configuration for the Copilot agent.
  - **target_repo** (string) - Required - The repository for the agent assignment.
  - **base_branch** (string) - Required - The base branch for the agent assignment.
  - **custom_instructions** (string) - Optional - Custom instructions for the agent.
  - **custom_agent** (string) - Optional - Custom agent to use.
  - **model** (string) - Optional - The model to use for the agent.

### Request Example
```json
{
  "assignees": ["copilot-swe-agent[bot]"],
  "agent_assignment": {
    "target_repo": "OWNER/REPO",
    "base_branch": "main",
    "custom_instructions": "",
    "custom_agent": "",
    "model": ""
  }
}
```

### Response
#### Success Response (201 Created)
- **assignees** (array of objects) - Information about the assigned users.
- **url** (string) - The URL of the assignees endpoint.

#### Response Example
```json
{
  "message": "Successfully added assignees.",
  "assignees": [
    {
      "login": "copilot-swe-agent[bot]",
      "id": 123456789,
      "node_id": "MDQ6VXNlcjEyMzQ1Njc4OQ==",
      "avatar_url": "https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/123456789?v=4",
      "html_url": "https://github.com/copilot-swe-agent[bot]",
      "type": "bot",
      "site_admin": false
    }
  ],
  "url": "https://api.github.com/repos/OWNER/REPO/issues/123/assignees"
}
```
```

--------------------------------

### Example Error Message for Protected Branch Update

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-protected-branches/about-protected-branches.md

This snippet shows an example error message that a collaborator might receive if they attempt to merge a pull request to a protected branch that has pending or rejected reviews.

```shell
remote: error: GH006: Protected branch update failed for refs/heads/main.
remote: error: Changes have been requested.

```

--------------------------------

### PUT /orgs/{org}/teams/{team_slug}/repos/{owner}/{repo}

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/data/reusables/organizations/team-api.md

Add or update a team's repository permission. You can add a repository to a team or update the team's permission level for an existing repository.

```APIDOC
## PUT /orgs/{org}/teams/{team_slug}/repos/{owner}/{repo}

### Description
Adds a repository to a team or updates the team's permission for a repository.

### Method
PUT

### Endpoint
`/orgs/{org}/teams/{team_slug}/repos/{owner}/{repo}`

### Parameters
#### Path Parameters
- **org** (string) - Required - The organization's name.
- **team_slug** (string) - Required - The URL-safe version of the team's name.
- **owner** (string) - Required - The owner of the repository (organization or user).
- **repo** (string) - Required - The name of the repository.

#### Request Body
- **permission** (string) - Required - The permission level to add or update. Accepted values are `pull` (Read) and `push` (Write).

### Request Example
```json
{
  "example": "PUT /orgs/my-org/teams/my-team/repos/my-org/my-repo"
}
```
```json
{
  "example": "{
    \"permission\": \"push\"
  }"
}
```

### Response
#### Success Response (204)
No content. Indicates the operation was successful.

#### Response Example
```json
{
  "example": ""
}
```
```

--------------------------------

### Using pre-commit hooks to prevent sensitive data commits

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/authentication/keeping-your-account-and-data-secure/removing-sensitive-data-from-a-repository.md

This example demonstrates a conceptual pre-commit hook setup using `git-secrets` to scan for and prevent the commitment of secrets. It requires collaborators to also set up the hook.

```shell
# Example conceptual pre-commit hook using git-secrets
# Ensure git-secrets is installed and configured
git secrets --scan
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
  echo "Error: Sensitive data detected. Commit aborted."
  exit 1
fi
exit 0
```

--------------------------------

### Export Repository List as JSON

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/code-security/codeql-for-vs-code/getting-started-with-codeql-for-vs-code/running-codeql-queries-at-scale-with-multi-repository-variant-analysis.md

This JSON snippet represents a custom list of repositories generated for variant analysis. It includes a list name and an array of repository names. This format is used for exporting and importing repository lists, facilitating collaboration and re-analysis.

```json
{
    "name": "new-repo-list",
    "repositories": [
        "my-organization/my-repository"
    ]
}
```

--------------------------------

### Dependabot Secrets API

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/rest/dependabot/secrets.md

You can create, update, delete, and retrieve information about encrypted secrets using the REST API. Authenticated users must have collaborator access to a repository to create, update, or read secrets. GitHub Apps must have the `dependabot_secrets` permission to use these endpoints.

```APIDOC
## About Dependabot Secrets

You can create, update, delete, and retrieve information about encrypted secrets using the REST API. For more information, see [Storing credentials for Dependabot to use](/code-security/dependabot/working-with-dependabot/configuring-access-to-private-registries-for-dependabot#storing-credentials-for-dependabot-to-use).

{% data reusables.actions.actions-authentication %} {% data variables.product.prodname_github_apps %} must have the `dependabot_secrets` permission to use these endpoints. Authenticated users must have collaborator access to a repository to create, update, or read secrets.
```

--------------------------------

### SQL Query Suggestions in Azure Data Studio

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/copilot/how-tos/get-code-suggestions/get-ide-code-suggestions.md

This snippet demonstrates how GitHub Copilot can suggest SQL join conditions as you type. It requires a compatible version of Azure Data Studio and the GitHub Copilot extension. The input is an incomplete SQL query, and the output is an inline suggestion for the join condition.

```sql
SELECT [UserId], [Red], [Orange], [Yellow], [Green], [Blue], [Purple], [Rainbow]
FROM [Tag].[Scoreboard]
INNER JOIN
```

--------------------------------

### Track File Types with Git LFS

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/repositories/working-with-files/managing-large-files/configuring-git-large-file-storage.md

This command associates a specific file extension with Git Large File Storage. It modifies the local .gitattributes file. Ensure you commit this file to your repository for proper collaboration and archive handling.

```shell
git lfs track "*.psd"
> Tracking "*.psd"
```

--------------------------------

### Create a .gitignore file

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/get-started/git-basics/ignoring-files.md

This command creates a `.gitignore` file in the root directory of your Git repository. This file tells Git which files and directories to ignore when you make a commit. Changes to this file are typically committed to the repository to share ignore rules with collaborators.

```shell
touch .gitignore
```

--------------------------------

### Use Local Composite Action in Workflow

Source: https://github.com/github/docs/blob/main/content/actions/tutorials/create-actions/create-a-composite-action.md

This YAML snippet shows how to integrate a composite action defined within the same repository into a GitHub Actions workflow. It uses a relative path to reference the action and passes an input parameter 'who-to-greet'. The output 'random-number' from the action is then used in a subsequent step.

```yaml
on: [push]

jobs:
  hello_world_job:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    name: A job to say hello
    steps:
      - uses: {% data reusables.actions.action-checkout %}
      - id: foo
        uses: ./.github/actions/hello-world-composite-action
        with:
          who-to-greet: 'Mona the Octocat'
      - run: echo random-number "$RANDOM_NUMBER"
        shell: bash
        env:
          RANDOM_NUMBER: {% raw %}${{ steps.foo.outputs.random-number }}{% endraw %}
```
